nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving tributaries, small streams, and flooded forest margins of the Peruvian and upper Brazilian Amazon basin; soft, acidic, often tannin-stained blackwater and whitewater streams over fine sand and leaf litter

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel health)
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
smooth-edged caves or PVC pipe hiding spots

diet.notes: Agassiz’s Corydoras feeds at the bottom of the water column, sifting through the substrate for food as an opportunistic scavenger. Sinking pellets, wafers, and catfish tablets make reliable staple foods, supplemented with frozen or live offerings such as bloodworm, tubifex, daphnia, brine shrimp, and micro-worms. Blanched vegetables like zucchini and cucumber are also accepted. Although they forage naturally for uneaten food that drifts to the bottom, they should not be relied upon solely as a clean-up crew — dedicated feeding is necessary to ensure they receive adequate nutrition.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (neon, cardinal, ember, rummy-nose)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) with adequate space
avoidWith: boisterous fast-moving fish that outcompete at feeding

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by NasserHalaweh, 1425x1068. Live specimen in a planted aquarium with the whole fish in frame; clearly shows the diagnostic spotted/reticulated body patterning, barbels and fins on a healthy in-colour individual. Sharp and well-lit; only viable CC live photograph found.

summary: Agassiz’s Corydoras is a peaceful, shoaling armoured catfish from the Peruvian Amazon, prized for its attractive spotted patterning. An easy-care beginner species best kept in groups of six or more over fine sand. A 2024 revision proposes reclassification to Hoplisoma agassizii, though the older name remains in common trade use.
careNotes:
Agassiz’s Corydoras (Corydoras agassizii) is a small, robust armoured catfish native to the Peruvian and upper Brazilian Amazon. A group of at least 6 individuals is strongly recommended — smaller groups show stress, reduced activity, and increased susceptibility to disease. Fine sand substrate is essential: Corydoras forage by sifting sand through their gills, and coarse gravel can damage delicate barbels, leading to infection. Keep the substrate clean with regular siphoning, as uneaten food and detritus degrade water quality rapidly in bottom-layer dead zones.

Water quality should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15). Tannin-stained water achieved through driftwood and leaf litter is ideal and reflects the natural blackwater biotope. Temperature range is 22–26°C; avoid elevated temperatures as Corydoras are more sensitive to heat than many tropical fish.

Agassiz’s Cory is frequently confused with Schwartz’s Cory (C. schwartzi) and Loxozonus Cory (C. loxozonus) due to overlapping spot patterns and variable individual markings; reliable ID requires examination of snout length, spot arrangement, and geographic provenance. All three species share similar care requirements.

A 2024 molecular phylogenetic revision proposes reclassification of the C. agassizii group into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma as H. agassizii; this revision is pending wider adoption and the name Corydoras agassizii remains in common trade use.

Corydoras are considered highly salt-sensitive and should not be kept in tanks where salt is regularly dosed. Copper-based medications are also harmful to the species; copper-free treatments should be used instead. Corydoras should not be relied upon as a clean-up crew substitute — they require their own dedicated feeding regimen.
breedingNotes: Corydoras agassizii can be bred in captivity with moderate effort. A temperature drop of 2–3°C simulating the onset of Amazonian cool-season rains is a reliable spawning trigger, often combined with increased water changes using slightly cooler, softer water. A 2:1 male-to-female ratio is typical. Eggs are adhesive and deposited on plant leaves, tank glass, or fine-leaved plants such as Java moss. Breeding adults may consume eggs; remove eggs or parents after spawning. Eggs hatch in 4–5 days at 24°C; fry are free-swimming within a further 2–3 days. First foods: infusoria, micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food. Breeding is considered moderately straightforward when conditioning and trigger protocols are followed, but tends to be less reliably spontaneous than more commonly kept species such as C. paleatus or C. aeneus.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras agassizii
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Peru, upper Amazon tributaries), water parameters (temperature 20–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm), adult size (males to ~5.5 cm, females to ~7 cm), diet, compatibility, substrate requirements, and breeding notes. Also noted similarity to C. schwartzi and C. loxozonus and identification challenges.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras agassizii
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), native range (Peru and Brazil, Amazon basin), adult size (to 6.9 cm TL), temperature range (22–26°C), and diet classification (omnivore, benthic). Confirmed geographic distribution includes Ucayali and Maraon river systems in Peru.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-focused husbandry guide covering Corydoras genus. Confirmed minimum group size of 6, fine sand substrate requirement, feeding protocols (sinking foods, live/frozen supplements), salt sensitivity, and copper medication caution applicable to C. agassizii. Species-specific parameters corroborated.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras species profiles
additional.notes: Covered Corydoras identification challenges, specifically the confusion between C. agassizii, C. schwartzi, and C. loxozonus due to variable spot patterns. Also noted the softwater Peruvian biotope preference and the importance of barbel health via fine substrate. Breeding trigger (cool water change method) corroborated.

additional.name: Dias, A.C. & Britto, M.R. (2024) — Hoplisoma reclass (molecular phylogeny reference)
additional.notes: Reference for the 2024 Dias & Britto molecular phylogenetic revision proposing reclassification of C. agassizii to Hoplisoma agassizii. Name Corydoras agassizii remains in active trade use; Hoplisoma agassizii noted as pending synonym. Surfaced via FishBase nomenclature records.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg scatterer; spawning triggered by cool water changes (2–3°C drop) and increased water flow. Eggs deposited on glass or plants; hatch in 4–5 days. Remove eggs post-spawning to prevent predation by parents. Fry raised on infusoria and micro-worms.
